subject: Physics
q: The pitch of a screw gauge is 0.5 mm and there are 100 divisions on its circular scale. The instrument reads +2 divisions when nothing is put in-between its jaws. In measuring the diameter of a wire, there are 8 divisions on the main scale and 83rd division coincides with the reference line. Then the diameter of the wire is
question: The pitch of a screw gauge is 0.5 mm and there are 100 divisions on its circular scale. The instrument reads +2 divisions when nothing is put in-between its jaws. In measuring the diameter of a wire, there are 8 divisions on the main scale and 83rd division coincides with the reference line. Then the diameter of the wire is
solution:
The least count is the minimal distance measured accurately with one division step of the circular scale.

Least Count is, $LC = \dfrac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Total number of divisions on circular scale}}$

The pitch of screw gauge is pitch = 0.5 mm and Circular Scale Divisions = 100.

$$LC = \dfrac{0.5\ \text{mm}}{100} = 0.005\ \text{mm}$$

It is given that when the jaws are empty, the instrument reads +2 divisions. Since this is a positive baseline value, it represents a positive zero error.

Zero Error = $+(\text{Zero Reading} \times LC)$

$$\text{Zero Error} = +2 \times 0.005\ \text{mm} = +0.01\ \text{mm}$$

There are 8 divisions fully visible on main scale. Since each full rotation on the main scale corresponds to the pitch value interval (0.5 mm per rotation):

So, the main scale reading is $MSR = 8 \times 0.5\ \text{mm} = 4.0\ \text{mm}$

The 83rd division aligns with the reference baseline.

So, the circular scale reading is $CSR = 83 \times LC = 83 \times 0.005\ \text{mm} = 0.415\ \text{mm}$

Observed Reading = $MSR + CSR$

$$\text{Observed Reading} = 4.0\ \text{mm} + 0.415\ \text{mm} = 4.415\ \text{mm}$$

To find the real measurement, we subtract the positive zero error value from observed reading:

True Diameter = $\text{Observed Reading} - \text{Zero Error}$

$$\text{True Diameter} = 4.415\ \text{mm} - 0.01\ \text{mm} = 4.405\ \text{mm}$$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q:
Assertion: A screw gauge having a smaller value of pitch has greater accuracy.

Reason: The least count of screw gauge is directly proportional to the number of divisions on circular scale.
question:
Assertion: A screw gauge having a smaller value of pitch has greater accuracy.

Reason: The least count of screw gauge is directly proportional to the number of divisions on circular scale.
options: Both Assertion and Reason are true and the Reason is correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is true, but the Reason is false.
Assertion is false but the Reason is true.
solution:
The accuracy of a screw gauge is depending on its resolution, which is its Least Count (LC). A smaller least count means the instrument can measure finer variations in length, which decides precision and accuracy.

$$ \mathrm{LC}=\frac{\text { Pitch }}{\text { Number of circular scale divisions }} $$

Keeping the number of circular divisions constant, if the pitch decreases (Pitch ↓ ), the least count also decreases (LC ↓). A smaller least count translates to higher accuracy/precision.

Therefore, the Assertion is True.

$$ \mathrm{LC} \propto \frac{1}{\text { Number of circular scale divisions }} $$

So, the least count (LC) is inversely proportional to the number of circular scale divisions, not directly proportional. Increasing the number of divisions reduces the least count value.

Therefore, the statement given in the reason is false.

Since the Assertion is true but the Reason is a false statement, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: In a specially designed vernier calipers, 10 divisions of the vernier scale are equal to 4 divisions of the main scale. In the following figure, the zero error and a measurement are shown. In the zero error, the 5th and the 10th divisions of the vernier coincides with 1 mm and 3 mm mark on the main scale respectively and in the measurement, the 2nd and the 7th divisions of the vernier scale coincide with the 23 mm and 25 mm mark on the main scale respectively as shown in the figure. Report the length measured with due regards to the error.
question: In a specially designed vernier calipers, 10 divisions of the vernier scale are equal to 4 divisions of the main scale. In the following figure, the zero error and a measurement are shown. In the zero error, the 5th and the 10th divisions of the vernier coincides with 1 mm and 3 mm mark on the main scale respectively and in the measurement, the 2nd and the 7th divisions of the vernier scale coincide with the 23 mm and 25 mm mark on the main scale respectively as shown in the figure. Report the length measured with due regards to the error.
solution:
The numbers 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 on the main scale represent centimeters, and each centimeter is divided into 10 equal

parts. Thus, each small division on the main scale is 1 millimeter.

$$1\ \mathrm{MSD} = 1\ \mathrm{mm}$$

According to the problem, 10 divisions of the vernier scale equal 4 divisions of the main scale:

$$10\ \mathrm{VSD} = 4\ \mathrm{MSD}$$

$$1\ \mathrm{VSD} = \frac{4}{10}\ \mathrm{MSD} = 0.4\ \mathrm{MSD} = 0.4\ \mathrm{mm}$$

After making required adjustment by shifting Vernier scale to the right so that the very Vernier scale division coincide with any main scale division. Here $2^{\text{nd}}$ division of VSD coincide with first division of MSD.

So, the least count is, $\mathrm{LC} = 1\mathrm{MSD} - 2\mathrm{VSD} = (1 - 2 \times 0.4)\mathrm{mm} = 0.2\ \mathrm{mm}$

It is given that during the zero error check, the $5^{\text{th}}$ vernier division coincides with the $1\ \mathrm{mm}$ mark, and the $10^{\text{th}}$ vernier division coincides with the $3\ \mathrm{mm}$ mark.

Position of $5^{\text{th}}$ Vernier Division = $V_0 + 5 \times \mathrm{VSD}$, where $V_0$ is the position of Vernier’s zero.

$$V_0 + 5 \times \mathrm{VSD} = 1\ \mathrm{mm}$$

$$V_0 + 5 \times (0.4\ \mathrm{mm}) = 1\ \mathrm{mm}$$

$$V_0 + 2.0\ \mathrm{mm} = 1\ \mathrm{mm}$$

$$V_0 = 1\ \mathrm{mm} - 2.0\ \mathrm{mm} = -1.0\ \mathrm{mm}$$

Since $V_0 = -1.0\ \mathrm{mm}$, the zero of the vernier scale lies to the left of the main scale zero. This means we have a negative zero error:

Zero Error = $-1.0\ \mathrm{mm}$

When measuring the object, the $2^{\text{nd}}$ vernier division coincides with the $23\ \mathrm{mm}$ mark, and the $7^{\text{th}}$ vernier division coincides with the $25\ \mathrm{mm}$ mark.

The vernier scale's zero during this measurement = $V_{\mathrm{meas}}$

$$\frac{23 - V_{\mathrm{meas}}}{2 - 0} = \frac{25 - 23}{7 - 2}$$

$$\frac{23 - V_{\mathrm{meas}}}{2} = \frac{2}{5} \Rightarrow 23 - V_{\mathrm{meas}} = \frac{4}{5}$$

$$\Rightarrow V_{\mathrm{meas}} = (23 - 0.8)\ \mathrm{mm}$$

Thus, the main scale reading is = $22.2\ \mathrm{mm}$

The second Vernier division coincides with the $23\ \mathrm{mm}$ mark on main scale. So Vernier scale reading is

$$\mathrm{VSR} = n \times \mathrm{LC} = 2 \times 0.6\ \mathrm{mm} = 1.2\ \mathrm{mm}$$

So, the observed reading is Reading = MSR + VSR = $22.2 + 1.2 = 23.4\ \mathrm{mm}$

The maximum permissible error in a measurement taken by a Vernier calliper is equal to its Least Count (LC).

Uncertainty = $\pm \mathrm{LC} = \pm 0.2\ \mathrm{mm}$

Therefore, reporting the measured length with its corresponding uncertainty gives $=(23.4 \pm 0.2) \mathrm{mm}$.

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: The pitch of a screw gauge having 50 divisions on its circular scale is 1 mm. When the two jaws of the screw gauge are in contact with each other, the zero of the circular scale lies 6 divisions below the line of graduation. When a wire is placed between the jaws, 3 linear scale divisions are clearly visible while 31st division on the circular scale coincides with the reference line. The diameter of the wire is
question: The pitch of a screw gauge having 50 divisions on its circular scale is 1 mm. When the two jaws of the screw gauge are in contact with each other, the zero of the circular scale lies 6 divisions below the line of graduation. When a wire is placed between the jaws, 3 linear scale divisions are clearly visible while 31st division on the circular scale coincides with the reference line. The diameter of the wire is
solution:
The least count of a screw gauge is the distance moved by the spindle when the circular scale is rotated by one small division.

Least Count is $\mathrm{LC}=\frac{\text { Pitch }}{\text { Total number of divisions on circular scale }}$

The pitch of given screw gauge is Pitch $=1 \mathrm{~mm}$, Circular Scale Divisions $=50$.

$$ \mathrm{LC}=\frac{1 \mathrm{~mm}}{50}=0.02 \mathrm{~mm} $$

When the two jaws are in contact, the zero of the circular scale lies 6 divisions below the reference line of graduation.

Because the circular scale zero hasn't reached the reference line yet, the instrument is registering a baseline reading lesser than zero before any object is inserted. This is a negative zero error.

Zero Error $=-($ Zero Reading × LC$)$

Zero Error $=-6 \times 0.02 \mathrm{~mm}=-0.12 \mathrm{~mm}$

When the wire is placed between the jaws linear scale divisions are clearly visible. Since each main scale division corresponds to 1 mm (the pitch value):

So, main scale reading is,

$$ \mathrm{MSR}=3 \times 1 \mathrm{~mm}=3 \mathrm{~mm} $$

The 31st division on circular scale coincides with the reference line.

So, the circular scale reading is $\mathrm{CSR}=31 \times \mathrm{LC}=31 \times 0.02 \mathrm{~mm}=0.62 \mathrm{~mm}$

Observed Reading $=$ MSR + CSR

Observed Reading $=3 \mathrm{~mm}+0.62 \mathrm{~mm}=3.62 \mathrm{~mm}$

To find the actual measurement, we subtract the zero error from the observed reading :

True Diameter $=$ Observed Reading - Zero Error

True Diameter $=3.62 \mathrm{~mm}-(-0.12 \mathrm{~mm})=3.74 \mathrm{~mm}$

Therefore, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: The distance moved by the screw of a screw gauge is 2 mm in four rotations and there are 50 divisions on its cap. When nothing is put between its jaws, 30th division of circular scale coincides with reference line, with zero of circular scale lying above the reference line. When a plate is placed between the jaws, main scale reads 2 divisions and circular scale reads 20 divisions. Thickness of plate is
question: The distance moved by the screw of a screw gauge is 2 mm in four rotations and there are 50 divisions on its cap. When nothing is put between its jaws, 30th division of circular scale coincides with reference line, with zero of circular scale lying above the reference line. When a plate is placed between the jaws, main scale reads 2 divisions and circular scale reads 20 divisions. Thickness of plate is
solution:
The pitch of a screw gauge is defined as linear distance by the screw per complete rotation.

$$\text{Pitch} = \frac{\text{Total distance moved}}{\text{Number of full rotations}}$$

The screw moves 2 mm in 4 rotations:

$$\text{Pitch} = \frac{2\ \text{mm}}{4} = 0.5\ \text{mm}$$

The Least Count (LC) is the smallest distance that can be measured accurately with the instrument.

$$\text{Least Count LC} = \frac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Total number of divisions on the circular scale}}$$

$$\text{LC} = \frac{0.5\ \text{mm}}{50} = 0.01\ \text{mm}$$

When the jaws are closed and no object is present, the zero mark of the circular scale should align with the reference line. If it does not, a zero error exists.

It is given that the zero of the circular scale lies above the reference line, and the 30th division coincides with the line.

When the circular zero is above the reference line, the instrument has rotated too far inward. It registers a reading even though no object is present. This is a negative zero error, meaning the instrument reads less than it should during an actual measurement.

$$\text{Positive zero error} = (\text{Total Divisions} - \text{Coinciding Division}) \times \text{LC}$$

$$\text{Negative zero error} = -(\text{Coinciding Division}) \times \text{LC}$$

Using Negative zero error

$$= -(30) \times 0.01\ \text{mm} = -30 \times 0.01\ \text{mm} = -0.3\ \text{mm}$$

When the plate is introduced, the main scale reads 2 divisions and the circular scale reads 20 divisions.

One main scale division (MSD) equals the pitch when the screw advances 1 division per rotation.

Thus, $1\ \text{MSD} = 0.5\ \text{mm}$.

$$\text{Main Scale Reading MSR} = 2 \times 0.5\ \text{mm} = 1.0\ \text{mm}$$

$$\text{Circular Scale Reading CSR} = \text{Coinciding division} \times \text{LC} = 20 \times 0.01\ \text{mm} = 0.2\ \text{mm}$$

Observed Reading = MSR + CSR = $1.0\ \mathrm{mm}$ + $0.2\ \mathrm{mm}$ = $1.2\ \mathrm{mm}$

The true measurement is obtained by subtracting the zero error (with its sign) from the observed measurement:

True Thickness = Observed Reading $-$ Zero Error

$$\mathrm{True\ Thickness} = 1.2\ \mathrm{mm} - (-0.3\ \mathrm{mm})$$

$$\mathrm{True\ Thickness} = 1.2\ \mathrm{mm} + 0.3\ \mathrm{mm} = 1.5\ \mathrm{mm}$$

Therefore, the true thickness of the plate is $1.5\ \mathrm{mm}$. Thus, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: The pitch of a screw gauge is 0.05 cm. In how many revolutions of hollow cylinder the screw will advance 0.35 cm in the straight line?
question: The pitch of a screw gauge is 0.05 cm. In how many revolutions of hollow cylinder the screw will advance 0.35 cm in the straight line?
solution:
Pitch of a screw gauge means the distance moved by the screw in one complete revolution.

Given,

$$\text{Pitch} = 0.05\ \text{cm}$$

Distance to be advanced:

$$0.35\ \text{cm}$$

Number of revolutions

$$= \frac{\text{Distance moved}}{\text{Pitch}} = \frac{0.35}{0.05} = 7$$

So, the hollow cylinder must be rotated $7$ times.

Correct option: A. $7$

subject: Physics
q:
Rank the vernier callipers/screw gauge in the order of their increasing precision:

1. Screw gauge having main scale division 1 mm and no. of circular division = 200.

2. Screw gauge having main scale division 0.5 mm and no. of circular division = 400.

3. Vernier callipers having main scale division = 1 mm such that 9 MSL = 10 VSD.

4. Vernier callipers having main scale division = 1 mm such that 4 MSL = 5 VSD.
question:
Rank the vernier callipers/screw gauge in the order of their increasing precision:

1. Screw gauge having main scale division 1 mm and no. of circular division = 200.

2. Screw gauge having main scale division 0.5 mm and no. of circular division = 400.

3. Vernier callipers having main scale division = 1 mm such that 9 MSL = 10 VSD.

4. Vernier callipers having main scale division = 1 mm such that 4 MSL = 5 VSD.
solution:
The Least Count (LC) is the smallest value that can be measured by a measuring instrument. The smaller the Least Count, the more precise the instrument is.

  • For a Screw Gauge: The Least Count is calculated as

$$ L=\frac{\text { Pitch }}{\text { Number of circular scale division }} $$

The pitch is the same as the smallest main scale division.

  • For a Vernier Calliper: The Least Count is the difference between one Main Scale Division (MSD) and one Vernier Scale Division (VSD). $L C=1$ MSD-1VSD.

We have four different tools. To "rank them in order of increasing precision," we need to find their Least Counts. The tool with the largest Least Count is the least precise, and the one with the smallest Least Count is the most most precise.

Screw Gauge (1)

Pitch=1mm; Divisions = 200 .

$$ L C_1=\frac{1 \mathrm{~mm}}{200}=0.005 \mathrm{~mm} $$

Screw Gauge (2)

Pitch $=0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$; Divisions $=400$.

$$ L C_2=0.5 \frac{\mathrm{~mm}}{400}=0.00125 \mathrm{~mm} $$

Vernier Callipers (3)

$1 \mathrm{MSD}=1 \mathrm{~mm}$.

It is given that $10 \mathrm{VSD}=9 \mathrm{MSD}$, so $1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{9}{10} \mathrm{MSD}=0.9 \mathrm{~mm}$.

$$ L C_3=1 \mathrm{~mm}-0.9 \mathrm{~mm}=0.1 \mathrm{~mm} $$

Vernier Callipers (4)

$1 \mathrm{MSD}=1 \mathrm{~mm}$.

It is given that $5 \mathrm{VSD}=4 \mathrm{MSD}$, so $1 \mathrm{VSD}=45 \mathrm{MSD}=0.8 \mathrm{~mm}$.

$$ L C_4=1 \mathrm{~mm}-0.8 \mathrm{~mm}=0.2 \mathrm{~mm} $$

So, $L C_4(0.2 \mathrm{~mm})$ is the largest. $L C_3(0.1 \mathrm{~mm})$ is next. $L C_1(0.005 \mathrm{~mm})$ is much smaller. $L C_2(0.00125 \mathrm{~mm})$ is the smallest of all.

So the order of increasing precision is $4<3<1<2$.

Correct Option : (D) $\mathbf{4}<\mathbf{3}<\mathbf{1}<\mathbf{2}$

subject: Physics
q: A screw gauge has 50 divisions on its circular scale. The circular scale is 4 units ahead of the pitch scale marking, prior to use. Upon one complete rotation of the circular scale, a displacement of 0.5 mm is noticed on the pitch scale. The nature of zero error involved and the least count of the screw gauge are respectively
question: A screw gauge has 50 divisions on its circular scale. The circular scale is 4 units ahead of the pitch scale marking, prior to use. Upon one complete rotation of the circular scale, a displacement of 0.5 mm is noticed on the pitch scale. The nature of zero error involved and the least count of the screw gauge are respectively
solution:
The circular scale is 4 units ahead of the pitch scale when not measuring anything. This means the instrument already shows a small positive reading, even though nothing is measured.

Zero error is the difference between what the screw gauge reads and what the true measurement should be (which is zero if nothing is measured).

The error is calculated by multiplying the extra divisions (4) by the least count (L.C.), like this:

$$ \text{Error} = 4 \times \text{L.C.} $$

Because this value is greater than zero, the zero error is positive.

The least count (L.C.) tells us the smallest value the screw gauge can measure. It is found by dividing the distance moved on one full rotation (pitch) by the total number of circular scale divisions.

$$ L.C. = \frac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Number of circular scale divisions}} $$

$$ L.C. = \frac{0.5~\mathrm{mm}}{50} = 0.01~\mathrm{mm} = 10~\mu\mathrm{m} $$

So, the least count (L.C.) is $10~\mu$ m.

Therefore, the screw gauge has a positive zero error and the least count is $10~\mu$m. The correct choice is option (B).

subject: Physics
q: In a Screw Gauge, fifth division of the circular scale coincides with the reference line when the ratchet is closed. There are 50 divisions on the circular scale, and the main scale moves by 0.5 mm on a complete rotation. For a particular observation the reading on the main scale is 5 mm and the 20th division of the circular scale coincides with reference line. Calculate the true reading.
question: In a Screw Gauge, fifth division of the circular scale coincides with the reference line when the ratchet is closed. There are 50 divisions on the circular scale, and the main scale moves by 0.5 mm on a complete rotation. For a particular observation the reading on the main scale is 5 mm and the 20th division of the circular scale coincides with reference line. Calculate the true reading.
solution:
The distance moved by the screw in one full turn 0.5 mm . This means, the main scale moves 0.5 mm in one rotation, so the Pitch is 0.5 mm .

Pitch $=0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$

Number of divisions $=50$

$\mathrm{LC}=\frac{\text { Pitch }}{\text { Number of divisions }}=\frac{0.5 \mathrm{~mm}}{50}$

$\mathrm{LC}=0.01 \mathrm{~mm}$

When the ratchet is closed, it should point to 0 , but it points to the 5 th division.

  • Zero Error $=+($ Division $\times$ LC $)$

  • Zero Error $=5 \times 0.01 \mathrm{~mm}=+0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$

Because it's positive, we will subtract this later to fix our measurement

Since the 5th division is showing when closed, we have a positive zero error. This means the instrument overcounts, so we must subtract this error from our final measurement to get the true measurement.

Total Reading $=$ Main Scale Reading $($ MSR $)+($ Circular Scale Reading $($ CSR $) \times$ LC $)$

  • Main Scale Reading (MSR) $=5 \mathrm{~mm}$

  • Circular Scale Reading $(\mathrm{CSR})=20$

So, observed Reading $=\mathrm{MSR}+(\mathrm{CSR} \times \mathrm{LC})$

Observed Reading $=5 \mathrm{~mm}+(20 \times 0.01 \mathrm{~mm})=5+0.20=5.20 \mathrm{~mm}$

Finally, we correct the observed reading by removing the zero error.

True Reading $=$ Observed Reading - Zero Error

True Reading $=5.20 \mathrm{~mm}-0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$

True Reading $=5.15 \mathrm{~mm}$

Therefore, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: The main scale of Vernier Callipers reads in millimetre and its Vernier is divided into 8 divisions, which coincide with 5 divisions of main scale. When two jaws of instrument touch each other the zero of the Vernier coincide with the zero of main scale. A rod is tightly placed along its length between both jaws, it is observed that the zero of Vernier scale lies just left to 36th division of main scale and fourth division of Vernier scale coincide with the main scale. Then the measured value is:
question: The main scale of Vernier Callipers reads in millimetre and its Vernier is divided into 8 divisions, which coincide with 5 divisions of main scale. When two jaws of instrument touch each other the zero of the Vernier coincide with the zero of main scale. A rod is tightly placed along its length between both jaws, it is observed that the zero of Vernier scale lies just left to 36th division of main scale and fourth division of Vernier scale coincide with the main scale. Then the measured value is:
solution:
The smallest unit on the main scale is the main scale division (MSD). Here, $1 M S D=1 m m$.

We are told that 8 Vernier divisions coincide with 5 Main Scale divisions.

$$ 8 V S D=5 M S D $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ 1 V S D=\frac{5}{8} M S D=\frac{5}{8} \times 1 \mathrm{~mm}=0.625 \mathrm{~mm} $$

Least Count (LC) is the smallest measurement the instrument can handle. It is calculated as :

$$ L C=1 \text { MSD }-1 \text { VSD (Vernier Scale Division) } $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $L C=1 M S D-1 V S D$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ L C=1 \mathrm{~mm}-0.625 \mathrm{~mm}=0.375 \mathrm{~mm} $$

The zero of the Vernier lies just left of the $36{ }^{\text {th }}$ division. This means it has passed the $35{ }^{\text {th }}$ division but hasn't reached 36 yet.

$$ M S R=35 \mathrm{~mm} $$

We are told the $4^{\text {th }}$ division of the Vernier coincides with the main scale.

Vernier coincide division ( n ) $=4$

The final value is the Main Scale Reading (MSR) plus the (Vernier Scale Reading × LC)

Total Value $=\mathrm{MSR}+(\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{LC})$

Total Value $=35 \mathrm{~mm}+(4 \times 0.375 \mathrm{~mm})$

Total Value $=35 \mathrm{~mm}+1.5 \mathrm{~mm}=36.5 \mathrm{~mm}$

Since the options are in cm,

$$ 36.5 \mathrm{~mm}=3.65 \mathrm{~cm} $$

Therefore, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge, 5 complete rotations of circular scale give 1.5 mm reading on linear scale. Circular scale has 50 divisions. Least count of the screw gauge is $0.1 \times \mathrm{mm}$. Find the value of 100x .
question: In a screw gauge, 5 complete rotations of circular scale give 1.5 mm reading on linear scale. Circular scale has 50 divisions. Least count of the screw gauge is $0.1 \times \mathrm{mm}$. Find the value of 100x .
solution:
The pitch is defined as the distance moved by the spindle per complete rotation of the circular scale.

$$\text{Pitch} = \frac{\text{Total distance moved on linear scale}}{\text{Number of complete rotations}}$$

Number of complete rotations, $N = 5$, the linear scale reading is $1.5\ \text{mm}$.

Substituting the given values:

$$\text{Pitch} = \frac{1.5\ \text{mm}}{5} = 0.3\ \text{mm}$$

The least count is the smallest distance the screw gauge can measure, which corresponds to the movement of the spindle when the circular scale is rotated by exactly one division.

$$\text{Least Count} = \frac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Total number of divisions on circular scale}}$$

The number of divisions on circular scale is $50$.

Substituting the values:

$$\text{LC} = \frac{0.3\ \text{mm}}{50}$$

$$\text{LC} = \frac{3}{500}\ \text{mm} = 0.006\ \text{mm}$$

$$0.006 = 0.1x$$

$$x = \frac{0.006}{0.1}$$

$$x = 0.06$$

Therefore, the value of $100x$ is $6$.

subject: Physics
q: The pitch of a screw gauge is $\mathbf{1 ~ m m}$ and there are $\mathbf{1 0 0}$ divisions on the circular scale. While measuring the diameter of a wire, the linear scale reads 1 mm and $47^{\text {th }}$ division on the circular scale coincides with the reference line. The length of the wire is 5.6 cm . The curved surface area (in $\mathrm{cm}^2$ ) of the wire is A. Then find the value of 100A ( In Nearest Integer ).
question: The pitch of a screw gauge is $\mathbf{1 ~ m m}$ and there are $\mathbf{1 0 0}$ divisions on the circular scale. While measuring the diameter of a wire, the linear scale reads 1 mm and $47^{\text {th }}$ division on the circular scale coincides with the reference line. The length of the wire is 5.6 cm . The curved surface area (in $\mathrm{cm}^2$ ) of the wire is A. Then find the value of 100A ( In Nearest Integer ).
solution:
The least count is the smallest distance measured accurately by one division step of the circular scale:

Least Count is LC = $$\frac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Total divisions on circular scale}}$$

The pitch of given Vernier scale is = 1 mm and circular divisions = 100.

$$\mathrm{LC} = \frac{1\ \mathrm{mm}}{100} = 0.01\ \mathrm{mm}$$

Linear scale reading is given as 1 mm.

The $47^{\text{th}}$ division coincides with the reference line.

$$\mathrm{CSR} = 47 \times \mathrm{LC} = 47 \times 0.01\ \mathrm{mm} = 0.47\ \mathrm{mm}$$

Total measured diameter is D = LSR + CSR = 1 mm + 0.47 mm = 1.47 mm

$$\mathrm{D} = \frac{1.47\ \mathrm{mm}}{10} = 0.147\ \mathrm{cm}$$

The length is given as 5.6 cm.

The curved surface of a wire forms a cylinder. The surface area formula is

$$\mathrm{A} = 2\pi R L$$

Since the diameter D = 2R, the expression simplifies to:

$$\mathrm{A} = \pi D L$$

Substituting values,

$$\mathrm{A} = \frac{22}{7} \times 0.147\ \mathrm{cm} \times 5.6\ \mathrm{cm}$$

$$\mathrm{A} = 22 \times 0.147 \times 0.8$$

$$\mathrm{A} = 22 \times 0.1176$$

$$\mathrm{A} = 2.5872\ \mathrm{cm}^2$$

$$\mathrm{A} \approx 2.59\ \mathrm{cm}^2$$

Therefore, the curved surface area of the wire is 2.59 cm$^2$.

$$\therefore $$ 100A = 259

subject: Physics
q: The length of the string of a simple pendulum is measured with a metre scale to be 90.01 cm . The radius of the bob plus the length of the hook is measured to be 2.132 cm using a screw gauge. The effective length of the pendulum is L? Then Fond the value of 100L. (This effective length is defined as the distance between the point of suspension and the center of the bob).
question: The length of the string of a simple pendulum is measured with a metre scale to be 90.01 cm . The radius of the bob plus the length of the hook is measured to be 2.132 cm using a screw gauge. The effective length of the pendulum is L? Then Fond the value of 100L. (This effective length is defined as the distance between the point of suspension and the center of the bob).
solution:
The effective length ($L_{\mathrm{eff}}$) of a simple pendulum is the total straight-line distance from the rigid point of suspension down to the center of mass (the center) of the spherical bob.

So, $L_{\mathrm{eff}}$ is the sum of length of string ($l$) and combined length of hook and bob radius ($r_{\mathrm{hook+bob}}$)

When adding measurements obtained from different experimental instruments, the rules of significant figures for addition:

Length of string ($l$) is 90.01 cm. This measurement has 2 decimal places.

Radius of (bob + hook) length is $r_{\mathrm{hook+bob}} = 2.132\ \mathrm{cm}$. This measurement has 3 decimal places

$$L_{\mathrm{eff}} = 90.01\ \mathrm{cm} + 2.132\ \mathrm{cm}$$

$$L_{\mathrm{eff}} = 92.142\ \mathrm{cm}$$

The rule for addition states that the final sum cannot have more digits to the right of the decimal point than the least precise measurement used in the calculation.

90.01 has 2 decimal places.

2.132 has 3 decimal places.

Therefore, final value must be rounded to exactly 2 decimal places:

$$L_{\mathrm{eff}} = 92.14\ \mathrm{cm}$$

Therefore, the effective length of the pendulum is 92.14 cm.

$$\therefore $$ 100$$L_{\mathrm{eff}}$$ = 9214

subject: Physics
q: The pitch of a screw gauge is 1 mm and there are 100 divisions on the circular scale. In measuring the diameter of a sphere there are six divisions on the linear scale and 40 divisions on circular scale coincides with the reference line. The diameter of the sphere is D. Then find the value of 100D.
question: The pitch of a screw gauge is 1 mm and there are 100 divisions on the circular scale. In measuring the diameter of a sphere there are six divisions on the linear scale and 40 divisions on circular scale coincides with the reference line. The diameter of the sphere is D. Then find the value of 100D.
solution:
A screw gauge converts a rotational movement into a precise linear movement.

Linear scale (main scale) is the fixed scale along the axis of the cylinder. It measures the major part of the distance.

Circular scale is the rotating scale on the thimble. It measures the fraction of distance added when the screw is rotated by less than one full turn.

The pitch is defined as the linear distance moved by the screw along the main axis when it is given one complete rotation. The pitch of given screw gauge is 1 mm which means one full 360-degree rotation of the circular scale moves the jaw forward by exactly 1 mm.

The least count is the absolute smallest measurement the instrument can accurately record.

$$LC = \frac{\text{Pitch}}{N}$$

Where N is the number of divisions on the circular scale, $N = 100$.

$$LC = \frac{1\ \text{mm}}{100} = 0.01\ \text{mm}$$

For every circular rotation by a single division, the instrument measures an additional 0.01 mm.

Since each division on the linear scale corresponds to one pitch (1 mm), and the number of main scale division is 6. So, the main scale reading is,

$$\text{MSR} = 6 \times 1\ \text{mm} = 6.00\ \text{mm}$$

The 40th division on the circular scale coincides with the reference line. So, the circular scale reading is,

$$\text{CSR} = 40 \times LC$$

$$\text{CSR} = 40 \times 0.01\ \text{mm} = 0.40\ \text{mm}$$

The total diameter is the sum of both readings.

$$\text{Total Reading} = \text{MSR} + \text{CSR}$$

$$\text{Total Reading} = 6.00\ \text{mm} + 0.40\ \text{mm} = 6.40\ \text{mm}$$

Therefore, the diameter(D) of the sphere is 6.40 mm.

$$\therefore $$ 100D = 640

subject: Physics
q: In a Vernier callipers, each cm on the main scale is divided into 20 equal parts. 10 Vernier scale divisions are equivalent to 9 main scale divisions. The value of Vernier constant will be ___________ $\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$
question: In a Vernier callipers, each cm on the main scale is divided into 20 equal parts. 10 Vernier scale divisions are equivalent to 9 main scale divisions. The value of Vernier constant will be ___________ $\times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm}$
solution:
we know, the formula for the vernier constant (vc) of a vernier caliper is,

$$V C=1 M S D-1 V S D$$

Where, MSD : main scale division VSD: Vernier scale division

Now, we can find, $M S D$ as $1 \mathrm{~cm}=20 \mathrm{MSD}$ (given) So $1~ M S D=\frac{1}{20} \mathrm{~cm}\quad$ .... (1)

Now, for $V S D$,

Given, $10 \mathrm{VSD}=9 \mathrm{MSD}$

So $1 V S D=\frac{9}{10} M S D$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { from eq (1), }\\ &1 ~V S D=\frac{9}{10} \times \frac{1}{20} \mathrm{~cm}=\frac{9}{200} \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Hence, } V C & =\left(\frac{1}{20}-\frac{9}{200}\right) \mathrm{cm} \\ V C & =\frac{1}{200}=5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~cm} . \\ \Rightarrow \quad V C & =5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mm} \end{aligned}$$
